data.title: Will AI-Driven Efficiency And Cash Returns Reshape Chord Energy’s (CHRD) Investment Narrative After Mixed Q4?
data.description:
• In recent days, Chord Energy reported Q4 2025 results with earnings per share below expectations but revenue ahead of forecasts, while simultaneously returning roughly half of adjusted free cash flow to shareholders via dividends and buybacks and insiders, including senior executives, sold shares as the stock traded near its 52-week high.
  • Behind the headlines, Chord Energy outperformed its full-year 2025 oil volume guidance, cut capital spending versus plan, and used AI-driven, longer-lateral wells to lift free cash flow efficiency, even as earlier crude price swings led to a very large collapse in net income.
  • Now we’ll examine how Chord Energy’s AI-driven efficiency gains and stronger free cash flow shape its existing investment narrative and risks.

Chord Energy Investment Narrative Recap

To own Chord Energy, you need to be comfortable with a focused Bakken story that leans heavily on AI-driven efficiency and free cash flow, but still lives and dies by commodity prices. The latest results reinforced that mix: operationally strong, with better oil volumes and lower capex, yet net income was hit hard by earlier crude price swings. Recent insider selling near 52-week highs is worth watching, but it does not appear to change the near term catalyst of converting efficiency gains into consistent cash returns, or the key risk of earnings fragility when prices move.

The most relevant recent development is Chord returning about 50% of adjusted free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks, alongside a 4.2% dividend yield. That capital return profile sits on top of efficiency gains from AI optimization and longer laterals, and it is central to the current bull case: if free cash flow holds up, capital returns can remain attractive, but if commodity or regulatory pressures bite, that payout framework could quickly look stretched.

Yet beneath the efficiency story and rising payouts, investors should be aware of how exposed Chord remains to tighter regulation and potential long term pressure on oil demand...

data.title: Novartis Adds Rare Disease Depth And Cardiovascular Optionality For Investors
data.description:
• Novartis received FDA approval for Cosentyx to treat pediatric hidradenitis suppurativa, expanding options for adolescent patients with this chronic skin disease.
  • The approval makes Cosentyx the first IL-17A inhibitor cleared for this pediatric indication in the United States.
  • Novartis also signed a licensing agreement with Unnatural Products to develop macrocyclic peptide therapeutics for cardiovascular diseases.
  • The deal targets previously hard-to-address cardiovascular drug targets and includes potential milestone opportunities.

For investors tracking SWX:NOVN, these updates add additional detail to an active year for the company. The share price stands at CHF119.5, with a return of 10.1% year to date and 25.3% over the past year. Over 3 and 5 years, the stock shows returns of 83.2% and 88.2%, providing context on how the market has treated Novartis over different time frames.

Readers may want to watch how Cosentyx adoption in pediatric hidradenitis suppurativa develops, and how regulators and clinicians respond to real-world use. The macrocyclic peptide agreement broadens Novartis's approach to cardiovascular R&D, so any future pipeline disclosures, clinical updates, or announced milestones from this collaboration could influence sentiment around SWX:NOVN.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Novartis by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Novartis.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Novartis following this latest news.

For current and prospective shareholders, this update sits at the intersection of product depth and capital deployment. The Cosentyx approval extends an existing immunology franchise into a younger HS population, which may help defend share against large-cap peers such as AbbVie, Johnson & Johnson and Pfizer in an area where treatment options for adolescents are limited. The macrocyclic peptide deal with Unnatural Products pushes Novartis further into difficult cardiovascular targets that have historically been hard to address with small molecules or standard biologics. Together, these moves indicate continued appetite to deploy capital into external science alongside internal R&D, even as the company takes on bond financing for the Avidity acquisition and runs a sizeable buyback program.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Novartis Narrative

  • The Cosentyx pediatric HS approval lines up with the narrative focus on advanced therapies and regulatory progress, adding another indication in an existing high-priority brand.
  • External deals in cardiovascular medicine could test assumptions that pipeline execution risk remains concentrated in oncology and immunology, rather than spread across newer modalities.
  • The macrocyclic peptide collaboration and rare-disease expansion are not clearly reflected in the existing discussion of future earnings drivers, which leans more on radioligand therapy and established brands.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Novartis to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts highlight at least 1 risk around financial position, with higher leverage from recent bond issues adding another layer to watch as Novartis funds deals and buybacks.
  • ⚠️ Competitive and pricing pressure in immunology and cardiovascular care could limit the financial impact of new indications if payers or rivals respond aggressively.
  • 🎁 The HS approval strengthens a marketed product with real-world data, which can sometimes be more impactful than an early-stage pipeline win for investors tracking cash flow.
  • 🎁 The Unnatural Products partnership targets difficult cardiovascular biology, adding optionality that sits alongside existing radioligand and gene-based programs.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to monitor early prescription trends and physician uptake for pediatric HS, particularly how quickly Cosentyx is written by dermatologists and how payers handle access for adolescents. On the R&D side, any disclosures on target selection, development timelines or milestone progress within the macrocyclic peptide collaboration will help gauge how material this partnership could become relative to the broader pipeline and the Avidity integration. It is also worth watching how these product moves intersect with balance sheet metrics after the recent bond issuance and as the three year buyback program progresses.

Approach 1: Axcelis Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what Axcelis Technologies' future cash generation is worth in today's dollars.

For Axcelis, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $105.9 million, with analysts providing a future free cash flow estimate of $231 million for 2023. Beyond the usual analyst horizon, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035, with annual projections such as $96.8 million in 2026 and $100.1 million in 2035, each discounted back to reflect time and risk.

Aggregating these discounted projections gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $35.03 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$85.74, the DCF output suggests the stock is around 144.8% above this model's fair value estimate. This indicates a comparatively rich valuation based on these assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Axcelis Technologies Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It reflects what the market is willing to pay today based on what it expects from future earnings and how risky those earnings appear to be.

Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to justify a lower P/E. Axcelis Technologies currently trades on a P/E of 21.91x. That sits below the broader Semiconductor industry average P/E of about 43.64x and also below the peer average of 59.65x. This may suggest the market is assigning it a lower valuation than many comparables.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio concept goes a step further. It estimates what a company’s P/E might be, given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and key risks. 3.9% earnings growth and stable 10.3% margin

    • On the other hand, the tiny shift in margin from 10.4% to 10.3% shows cost pressure is present. This is consistent with commentary about higher personnel costs and R&D potentially weighing on net margins.

  • Within FY 2025, H1 net income was €82.4 million on €738.3 million of revenue and H2 net income was €68.5 million on €726.4 million of revenue, while over the last year as a whole earnings grew 3.9% and the net margin sat at 10.3%.

    • H2 2025 net income of €68.5 million compared with €50.6 million in H2 2024 sits alongside trailing twelve month revenue of about €1.46b. This is close to the €1.6b revenue level analysts discuss for future years.
    • At the same time, trailing earnings growth of 3.9% is much lower than the roughly 9.9% yearly growth analysts reference for the next few years, so the current run rate leaves some distance between actual results and the more bullish long term expectations.

Bulls argue these steady margins and incremental earnings growth are the first step toward the richer long term story. If that view interests you it is worth seeing how they connect today’s numbers to the bigger picture 🐂 Virbac Bull Case

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company earns over and above the return that shareholders require, based on the equity cost, and then capitalizes those extra profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Credit Acceptance, the model uses a book value of about $142.66 per share and a stable book value estimate of $136.25 per share, both drawn from median figures over the past 5 years. P/B compares the market value of the company to its accounting net assets. It is often used for asset heavy sectors like metals and mining where earnings can be volatile or still in the early stages.
